reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Build runner compute types table (CPU/memory values) is verified exactly against the `COMPUTE_TYPES` array in `ProjectsList.tsx` — a rare example of a table that will stay accurate.
Project card detail claims (name, git URL, stage count, last deployed time, cost with trend indicator) are all confirmed in `ProjectCard` component source.

Project settings dropdown items (Configure Git deployments, Configure Runner, Configure GitHub Actions runner, Delete project) precisely match the conditional rendering logic in `ProjectOverview.tsx`, including the 'only when gitUrl is connected' qualifiers.
Stage sorting behavior — dev stacks last, then by `lastUpdateTime` — is correctly described and grounded in `sortedDeployedStages` sort logic in `ProjectOverview.tsx`.

Troubleshooting section addresses genuinely grounded issues: `dismissFailedStageMutation`, `deleteUndeployedStageMutation`, and the cost data 24-hour lag from `Explanations.tsx` (`costsPerStack` tooltip).
The delete-project warning (stages not auto-deleted) mirrors the confirmation modal copy in `ProjectOverview.tsx`: 'It's advised to delete them before continuing.'

FAQ mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS-service, and comparison questions appropriately. The 'Do stages share anything?' FAQ answer is sourced verbatim from `Explanations.tsx`: 'Stages are fully isolated and don't share resources with each other.'
No mandatory style violations: no YAML Stacktape config blocks, no `StacktapeConfig` type import, no fenced TypeScript blocks with `defineConfig`, no multi-command bash blocks, no marketing language.
problems: Access control section's named roles (Owner, Admin, Developer, Viewer) and their specific permission assignments are not grounded in the provided source files. The only role name appearing in the source is `VIEWER` (`role !== 'VIEWER'` in `ProjectsList.tsx`). Specific capabilities like 'Only Owners can delete the organization' and the four-column permission table are not supported by the provided source — only individual permission strings like `deployments:delete-non-production`, `projects:delete`, `org:manage-codebuild-settings`, `projects:update-settings` appear in the source.

The `deployments:delete-production` permission string cited in the access control section ('Developers cannot delete production stages (requires `deployments:delete-production`, granted only to Owner and Admin)') does not appear in the provided source files. Only `deployments:delete-non-production` is grounded.

Stage naming constraint '1–10 characters long, lowercase alphanumeric with dashes allowed' is asserted in both the Stages section and the FAQ but is not grounded in any of the three provided source files.

The sub-navigation tab names listed in 'Viewing stage details' (Overview, Logs, Metrics, Costs, Activity, Configuration) are not explicitly confirmed in the provided source. `subNavigationPaths` is imported and used in `ProjectOverview.tsx` but its values are not shown.

`stacktape project:create` CLI command is linked in the Step 1 walkthrough but does not appear in the provided source files. The CLI pages visible in the git status do not include a `project-create.mdx` entry.

FAQ claim 'Stacktape prevents concurrent deployments to the same stage' implies an enforced blocking mechanism. The source shows an `isInProgress` flag and UI indicator, but active deployment blocking is not explicitly confirmed in the provided source.
mandatoryFixes: Revise the Access Control section to ground only what the source confirms: the `VIEWER` role name exists (grounded); the permission strings `deployments:delete-non-production`, `projects:delete`, `org:manage-codebuild-settings`, `projects:update-settings` exist (grounded). For the full role/permission mapping, either link to the dedicated access control page and note the details are there, or clearly attribute the role names to Stacktape documentation rather than claiming them as source-grounded facts. Do not present a four-column role table that cannot be verified from the provided source files.

Remove or hedge the `deployments:delete-production` permission claim — this string does not appear in any provided source file. If the intent is to say production stages require elevated permissions, derive that only from `canDeleteNonProduction = hasPermission('deployments:delete-non-production')` as sourced.

Either remove the `stacktape project:create` CLI link in the walkthrough Step 1 or replace it with a CLI command that is verifiable. The git status lists `docs/docs/cli/org-create.mdx` and similar pages but no `project-create.mdx`. If the command does not exist, replace with CLI deploy instructions only.
optionalImprovements: Add a short note in the Access Control section directing readers to the dedicated Team and access control page (already in Related Features) since that page is the authoritative source for role definitions — framing this section as an 'overview' rather than a complete reference would reduce the risk of stale claims.

The stage naming constraint ('1–10 characters') is stated as fact in two places but not grounded in the provided source. Consider softening to 'stage names have a short character limit' or verifying against the CLI source (`src/commands/deploy/index.ts`) before asserting the exact bounds.

The sub-navigation tab list (Overview, Logs, Metrics, Costs, Activity, Configuration) should be verified against `subNavigationPaths` in `StageDetailsHeader.tsx` before the page ships. If those paths aren't confirmed, list the tabs more loosely ('tabs for logs, metrics, costs, and deployment history').

The FAQ entry on concurrent deployment prevention could be scoped to what the source confirms: 'the Console shows an in-progress indicator and the stage is locked for new operations while a deployment is running', rather than asserting a hard block.

Consider adding a one-sentence callout near the top linking to 'Connecting your AWS account' — new users reading this page for the first time may not realize that an AWS account must be connected to the organization before any stage can be deployed. This is the most common first-time blocker and currently only appears in Related Features at the bottom.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Project card description (cost trend indicator, stage count badge, Git URL, last deployed time) is accurately grounded in ProjectsList.tsx source — including the trend label logic (New spend, Flat, rising/falling percent).
Build runner compute type table exactly matches the COMPUTE_TYPES array in ProjectsList.tsx (CPU/memory values for all five tiers), including the CodeBuild settings gear icon and modal form fields.

Project settings dropdown items (Configure Git deployments, Configure Runner, Configure GitHub Actions runner, Delete project) are accurately described with correct conditionality (Git URL required for GitOps and GitHub runner options), grounded in ProjectOverview.tsx.
Dev-mode stacks appearing at the bottom of the stage sidebar is correctly sourced from the sortedDeployedStages sort logic in ProjectOverview.tsx.

Dismissing failed stages ('dismiss' vs 'delete') is correctly distinguished — the page accurately reflects that dismissFailedStageMutation removes Console history while deleteUndeployedStageMutation removes undeployed records, matching the two separate confirmation modals in the source.
The Warning callout about orphaned AWS resources when deleting a project before its stages is actionable and grounded in the source confirmation modal that warns when hasDeployedStages is true.
FAQ spans all three required categories (Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS service, comparison/choosing) and most answers are 2-4 sentence paragraphs with useful concrete detail.
Fuzzy project search and sort-by-most-recently-updated behavior are both accurately described from the source.
problems: The page references `stacktape project:create` at route `/cli/project-create` in the Step 1 walkthrough. This CLI command does not appear in the git status (no project-create.mdx file exists) and is not visible in any of the provided source files. If this command does not exist, directing readers to it actively misleads them trying to create projects from the CLI.

The access control roles table (Owner, Admin, Developer, Viewer with full capability descriptions) is not grounded in any of the provided console-app source files. The source only surfaces specific permission strings: `org:manage-codebuild-settings`, `projects:delete`, `projects:update-settings`, `deployments:delete-non-production`. Role names and the full permission matrix are not present in the provided sources — this entire table may be accurate but it cannot be verified and could misstate which roles hold which permissions.

The claim 'When you sign up, Stacktape creates a default personal organization tied to your email' is not grounded in any of the provided source files. None of the provided files show organization creation or signup flows.

Stage name constraint '1–10 characters long, lowercase alphanumeric with dashes allowed' is asserted as fact but is not present in the provided source files (no validation logic is shown). This is a precise constraint that readers will rely on.

The FAQ entry 'Stacktape prevents concurrent deployments to the same stage' is not grounded in the provided source files. The source shows per-stage in-progress state tracking and UI indicators but nothing about blocking a second deployment at the API or CLI level.

The page says 'You can also manage organizations from the CLI with stacktape org:create and stacktape org:list' — while these routes do appear in git status, the page also omits `stacktape org:delete` which is also present in git status, creating an inconsistent CLI reference.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or replace the `stacktape project:create` CLI reference in the Step 1 walkthrough. There is no `project-create.mdx` in the git-tracked CLI docs. Replace with the Console-only flow (the UI path is well-documented) and remove the CLI fallback line unless the command can be verified to exist.

Narrow or caveat the access control roles table. The provided source files expose only permission strings (`org:manage-codebuild-settings`, `projects:delete`, `deployments:delete-non-production`, `projects:update-settings`), not role names or the full permission matrix. Either link to a dedicated `/stacktape-console/team-and-access-control` page for the authoritative table, or explicitly caveat that the table is summarized from Console behavior and may not reflect all edge cases — and verify role names against the actual Console auth source before publishing.

Remove or source-verify the stage name constraint ('1–10 characters long, lowercase alphanumeric with dashes allowed'). This is a specific constraint a reader will act on (naming a production stage), and it is not present in the provided source files. If correct, cite or verify from the actual validation code; if unverifiable, replace with a note to check the Console validation feedback when creating a stage.
optionalImprovements: The BuildRunnerForm in ProjectsList.tsx also exposes an `ec2RunnerAdditionalBuildCommands` textarea field for EC2 runner additional build commands. The page only mentions the CodeBuild additional install commands. Consider adding a note that EC2 runners have a parallel 'additional build commands' setting in the same form.

The Step 1 walkthrough mentions a project can be created 'from a starter template, from an existing Git repository, or as an empty project' — this matches the NewProjectMenu component with `showAddEmptyProject` prop, but the source also shows `location='projects-page'` which may affect what options appear. Consider verifying whether all three creation paths are available from this specific location.

The 'switching organizations' section describes a dropdown in the Console header, but no header navigation source file is included. The description is plausible but unverified — consider softening to 'use the organization selector in the Console header' rather than prescribing click flow if the header component source wasn't reviewed.
Consider adding a brief note about the `org:delete` CLI command alongside `org:create` and `org:list` for consistency, since the route exists in git status.

The FAQ answer about 'no hard limit on stages per project' is accurate but the note about IAM role quota (1,000 per account) as the practical ceiling is drawn from memory/context rather than source files — worth flagging this as an AWS account limit rather than a Stacktape limit for precision.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph clearly explains the intended organization → project → stage hierarchy and is close to answer-first length.
The page is organized around practical Console tasks rather than only definitions.
The build runner compute type table is useful and appears grounded in the provided ProjectsList source.
Links to related CLI and Console docs are helpful for reader navigation.
problems: Large portions of the page are not grounded in the provided source files, including organization creation, default personal organizations, organization switching, billing ownership, budgets, guardrails, alert channels, Git provider integrations, starter templates, stage name validation, project name validation, role capability details, and most troubleshooting claims.

Several Console UI flows are invented or over-specific without source support, such as exact navigation paths for organization switching, project creation options, deployment setup guidance, stage detail tabs, danger-zone deletion, and context-menu behavior.

Multiple H2 sections are not self-contained for extraction. `How the hierarchy works`, `Walkthrough: creating a project and deploying a stage`, `Common tasks`, `Troubleshooting`, and `Related features` start directly with H3s or lists instead of a defining paragraph.

The `Access control` section is especially risky: the provided source only shows permission checks such as `projects:delete`, `projects:update-settings`, `deployments:delete-non-production`, and `org:manage-codebuild-settings`, plus a `VIEWER` role check. It does not support the full role table or permission boundaries.

The page repeatedly states strong behavioral guarantees that are not visible in source, such as stages sharing no resources, organization deletion not deleting AWS resources, concurrent deployments being blocked, and moving projects between organizations not being supported.
FAQ breadth is good, but many answers are unsupported by the supplied source and include AWS/account-limit details not present in the page sources.

The walkthrough is not reliably copyable from the provided Console source because the source shows UI controls and route behavior but not the full create-project/create-stage flow described in the draft.
Some claims are more definitive than the source allows, for example `Every resource, cost metric, alarm, and deployment lives within this hierarchy` and `No data or state leaks between stages`.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or rewrite every Stacktape-specific claim that is not grounded in the provided source files. Keep only source-supported Console behavior from ProjectsList.tsx, ProjectOverview.tsx, and Explanations.tsx, or add the missing console-app source files before documenting those flows.

Rewrite the `Access control` section to only describe permissions and roles visible in the provided source, or replace it with a short link to `/stacktape-console/team-and-access-control` without unsupported role capability tables.

Add self-contained opening paragraphs under each H2 section, especially `How the hierarchy works`, `Walkthrough`, `Common tasks`, `Troubleshooting`, and `Related features`. Each first paragraph should name the Stacktape Console concept explicitly.

Replace invented Console navigation details with source-grounded route/button language. For example, document the visible `Projects` page, `Create new project`, `Filter projects`, `Configure Codebuild settings`, `New stage`, `Configure Runner`, `Configure Git deployments`, `Configure GitHub Actions runner`, and `Delete project` controls only where those labels appear in source.

Rewrite or remove troubleshooting entries that are not grounded in source. The only source-supported failure/dismissal flow is the `Dismiss failed stage` modal that removes deployment history for the stage.

Correct over-specific deletion wording. The provided source supports that deleting a project with deployed stages shows a warning and allows `Proceed anyway`; it does not prove the full orphaned-resource behavior described in the draft.
optionalImprovements: Add literal Console routes where source supports them, such as `/projects`, `/projects/:projectName/:stage/overview`, `/projects/:projectName/git-ops`, and create-stage routes from ProjectOverview.tsx.
Shorten the walkthrough H2 to `Project setup` or `Stage setup` for better scannability.
Keep the build runner section because it is strongly grounded, but explain that organization-level CodeBuild settings are shown only when the user has `org:manage-codebuild-settings`.
Move unsupported role and billing concepts into related links unless their source files are included in the page input.

Make FAQ answers narrower and source-aligned: focus on what projects and stages mean, what project cards show, how filtering works, how undeployed and failed stages appear, and where build runner settings live.

verifierResults.summary: The draft contains several well-grounded project-list and stage-isolation details, but it also makes many Console workflow, organization, access-control, validation, and operational-behavior claims that are not supported by the provided Console source files. The highest-impact fixes are to narrow organization/access-control prose, remove unsupported name/limit rules, and avoid asserting unverified Console flows.

issues.statement: The Organizations section claims that organizations are the top-level boundary for billing and access control, that signup creates a default personal organization tied to the user's email, that users can create additional organizations, that organizations own AWS accounts/team roles/budgets/guardrails/alert channels/notification rules/Git providers/third-party integrations, and that a header dropdown switches organization context across projects, costs, alarms, and team members.
issues.reason: The provided source files only show that the Projects page reads a selected organization and scopes project queries/settings by `organizationId`. They do not support the broader organization lifecycle, ownership, billing, integrations, or header-switching claims.
issues.suggestedFix: Limit this page to what is supported: projects are queried within the selected organization, and organization-level CodeBuild settings are configurable from the Projects page when the user has permission. Move or remove the default-personal-organization, ownership list, and header-dropdown claims unless console-app organization/navigation source is added.

evidence.quote:
const selectedOrg = useGlobalStore((store) => store.selectedOrganization);
  const projectsQuery = trpc.projects.useQuery({ organizationId: selectedOrg!.id });

issues.statement: The Access control section defines four roles, exact scopes, role capabilities, project-scoped visibility, and permission boundaries such as Owners deleting organizations, Admins managing AWS accounts/guardrails, Developers deleting only non-production stages, and Viewers being read-only.
issues.reason: The provided sources expose a few permission checks and a general explanation that roles determine what members can view or modify, but they do not define the role matrix or the exact role-to-permission mappings in the draft.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace the detailed role matrix with a cautious summary such as: the Console uses roles and permissions to control access, and this page's source shows permissions for project settings, project deletion, non-production deletion, and organization CodeBuild settings. Link to the team/access-control page for details, or add the relevant permission source files.

evidence.quote: Each member can have a different role that determines what they can view and modify.

issues.statement: The draft states that project names must use lowercase letters, numbers, and dashes; stage names must be 1–10 characters, lowercase alphanumeric with dashes allowed; there is no hard limit on stages per project; and the 10-character stage limit exists because stage names become part of downstream AWS identifiers.
issues.reason: The provided source files explain what projects and stages are, and ProjectOverview derives a stack name from `projectName` and `stage`, but no provided file contains these validation rules, length limits, or rationale.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the specific validation and limit claims unless the validation source is added. Keep only the supported description that a project represents an application and stages are isolated deployments of a project.

evidence.quote: A project represents your application. It usually maps 1:1 to a Git repository.
Stage, sometimes also called "environment", is a running deployment of a project. For example, production , staging or testing.

issues.statement: The FAQ says Stacktape prevents concurrent deployments to the same stage and blocks a second deployment attempt until the first completes.
issues.reason: The provided source tracks stages with deployments in progress and passes in-progress state into the UI, but it does not show a blocking rule or enforcement path for concurrent deployment attempts.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the answer to the supported behavior: the Console tracks and displays stages with deployments in progress. Do not claim that a second deployment is blocked unless CLI/server enforcement source is provided.

issues.statement: The walkthrough says a Console project can be created from a starter template, from an existing Git repository, or as an empty project, and that creating a stage routes the user to deployment setup with Git or CLI-specific guidance.
issues.reason: The provided source supports a Projects page button labeled “Create new project” with `showAddEmptyProject`, and a Project view button labeled “New stage” that opens `CreateNewStageForm`. It does not include `NewProjectMenu` or `CreateNewStageForm` internals, so the starter-template, existing-repository, undeployed-record, and deployment-setup routing details are not grounded in the supplied files.
issues.suggestedFix: State only that the Projects page has a “Create new project” action and the Project view has a “New stage” action. Add the missing component sources if the draft needs to document the exact creation options and post-create flow.

evidence.quote:
<NewProjectMenu
              dropdownButtonText="Create new project"
              location="projects-page"
              key="1"
              showAddEmptyProject
              buttonCss={{ marginRight: '0px' }}
            />

<Button
              width="100%"
              height="34px"
              rootCss={{ margin: '5px 3px' }}
              icon={<BiPlus css={{ marginRight: '-35px', marginLeft: '-60px' }} size={22} />}
              visualType="primary"
              text="New stage"
              onClick={() => setIsCreateNewStageModalOpen(true)}
            />

issues.statement: The Viewing stage details section lists exact stage subpages and capabilities: Overview resource list/deployment status/last operation, real-time log streaming, CloudWatch metrics for all resources, per-resource costs, activity history, and configuration.
issues.reason: The provided ProjectOverview source routes deployed stages to `/overview` and imports `StageDetailsHeader` and `subNavigationPaths`, but the actual tab list and tab contents are not present in the supplied files. The detailed tab names and capabilities are therefore not grounded here.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the supported navigation claim that clicking a deployed stage opens its overview route. Remove or generalize the detailed tab list unless `StageDetailsHeader` and the relevant stage subpage source files are added.

issues.statement: The troubleshooting section says newly deployed stages may show zero cost until the next billing cycle report is processed.
issues.reason: The provided source says cost reports are generated daily from AWS Cost and Usage Reports and may take up to a day to appear. “Next billing cycle” implies a much longer billing-period delay and contradicts the daily/up-to-a-day wording.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the sentence to: “Cost reports are generated daily from AWS Cost and Usage Reports and may take up to a day to appear, so newly deployed stages can temporarily show zero cost.”

evidence.quote: Reports are generated daily from AWS Cost and Usage Reports and may take up to a day to appear.

issues.statement: The FAQ claims project moves between organizations are not supported in the Console, and that deleting an organization removes Console metadata but does not delete deployed AWS resources.
issues.reason: No provided source file covers moving projects between organizations or deleting organizations. These are Stacktape-specific Console behavior claims that should be grounded in organization/project server or Console source before publication.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove these FAQ entries or rewrite them to link readers to the appropriate Console/account management page without asserting unsupported behavior. Add organization deletion and project migration source files if these answers must remain.

positiveFindings: The project-card description is mostly grounded: the source shows project name, Git URL or fallback text, stage count, last deployed time, monthly cost, and a trend indicator.
The filtering and sorting claims for the Projects page are supported by `useFuzzySearchList` over project name/Git URL and sorting by the latest stage `lastUpdateTime`.
The stage-isolation explanation is supported by `Explanations.tsx`, which says stages are fully isolated and do not share resources.

The organization-level CodeBuild settings section is well grounded: the source includes the “Configure Codebuild settings” action, compute type options, base image field, and additional install commands field.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is useful and matches several visible Projects/ProjectOverview behaviors, but it makes many Console-specific claims that are not grounded in the provided console-app sources. The highest-impact issues are unsupported organization/access-control details, unsupported create/delete UI flows, and unsupported naming/limit rules.

issues.statement: “An organization is the top-level boundary for team collaboration, billing, and access control… Organizations own: Connected AWS accounts; Team members and their roles; Projects and all their stages; Budgets, guardrails, alert channels, and notification rules; Build runner settings; Git provider and third-party integrations.”
issues.reason: The provided Console sources for this page show projects being queried within a selected organization and show organization-level CodeBuild settings, but they do not document the broader ownership model for billing, AWS accounts, budgets, guardrails, alert channels, notification rules, Git providers, or third-party integrations. This is a broad Stacktape Console claim that needs console source grounding.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the Organizations section to facts visible in source: projects are loaded for the selected organization, and CodeBuild build-runner settings are stored on the selected organization. Remove or link elsewhere without enumerating unsupported ownership details unless the relevant organization/settings source files are added.

evidence.quote:
const selectedOrg = useGlobalStore((store) => store.selectedOrganization);
  const projectsQuery = trpc.projects.useQuery({ organizationId: selectedOrg!.id });

issues.statement: “When you sign up, Stacktape creates a default personal organization tied to your email.” and “What is the default organization created on signup?... a personal organization is automatically created using your email address.”
issues.reason: No provided source file covers signup behavior or default organization creation. This is a concrete Console behavior claim, but the supplied sources only cover Projects page behavior, project overview behavior, and explanation text.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the signup/default-organization claim, or replace it with a cautious organization definition grounded in selected-organization scoping from ProjectsList.

evidence.quote:
const selectedOrg = useGlobalStore((store) => store.selectedOrganization);
  const projectsQuery = trpc.projects.useQuery({ organizationId: selectedOrg!.id });

issues.statement: “You switch between organizations using the dropdown in the Console header… You can also create a new organization directly from this dropdown.”
issues.reason: The provided source files do not include header organization-switching UI or an organization creation dropdown. The only header-related source in the page is the Projects page headline actions and the NewProjectMenu for projects.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the organization-switching walkthrough, or rewrite as a general statement that the Projects page is scoped to the selected organization without describing a header dropdown flow.
